coredata.dc:title: Development of highly flexible electromagnetic interference shielding composites for electronic applications using Cobalt/ Hevea brasiliensis seed husk carbon dots/Bamboo microfibre-polyvinyl alcohol
coredata.dc:creator: Alshahrani, Hassan
Arun Prakash, V.R.
coredata.dc:description: In this present work a highly flexible electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding composite was prepared from industrial crop for advanced engineering applications. The EMI shielding composite product was further tested for its effectiveness on high frequency bands of 8–20 GHz. Through pyrolysis, the leftover rubber seed hulls were converted into carbon dots (C-dots). The composites were characterized after being made using the solution casting process. The inclusion of carbon dots increased the relative permittivity up to 9.4 with the lowest loss factor of 0.62 at 8 GHz, according to the findings. The hysteresis loop showed a broader "S" curve for cobalt with biochar at a 2 vol% concentration. Additionally, the composite contains 3 vol% of cobalt/carbon dots records a maximum tensile strength of 54.7 MPa, modulus of 0.88 GPa, and % of elongation of 128.7. However, the composite contains 5 vol% of cobalt/carbon dots (PBC4) had a total EMI shielding effect of − 54.3 dB at 20 GHz. These enhanced EMI shielding composites with highly retained flexibility could be employed as a protective material in telecommunication; chip sets manufacturing, defense and medical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biocomposite
Wood fibers
Carbon
Mechanical properties
Magnetic properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ball milling of Co and C-dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code ( NU/NRP/SERC/11/12 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: C-dots preparation route and confirmation reports.
PVA Co/C-dots flexible composite sheet prepared.
PVA Co/C-dots flexible composite sheet prepared.
Total wave shielding results of proposed films.
Real time Co/C-dots and BMF-PVA composite strip.
SEM image of Bamboo fiber and Co/C-dots toughened PVA composites.

head.ce:title: Development of highly flexible electromagnetic interference shielding composites for electronic applications using Cobalt/ Hevea brasiliensis seed husk carbon dots/Bamboo microfibre-polyvinyl alcohol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, College of Engineering, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, College of Engineering, Najran University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Najran University, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, J.N.N Institute of Engineering, Chennai, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, J.N.N Institute of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science, J.N.N Institute of Engineering, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Composite Research Lab, Metro Composites, Chennai, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Polymer Composite Research Lab, Metro Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymer Composite Research Lab, Metro Composites, Chennai, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Mechanical Engineering, J.N.N Institute of Engineering, Chennai, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, J.N.N Institute of Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this present work a highly flexible electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding composite was prepared from industrial crop for advanced engineering applications. The EMI shielding composite product was further tested for its effectiveness on high frequency bands of 8–20 GHz. Through pyrolysis, the leftover rubber seed hulls were converted into carbon dots (C-dots). The composites were characterized after being made using the solution casting process. The inclusion of carbon dots increased the relative permittivity up to 9.4 with the lowest loss factor of 0.62 at 8 GHz, according to the findings. The hysteresis loop showed a broader "S" curve for cobalt with biochar at a 2 vol% concentration. Additionally, the composite contains 3 vol% of cobalt/carbon dots records a maximum tensile strength of 54.7 MPa, modulus of 0.88 GPa, and % of elongation of 128.7. However, the composite contains 5 vol% of cobalt/carbon dots (PBC4) had a total EMI shielding effect of − 54.3 dB at 20 GHz. These enhanced EMI shielding composites with highly retained flexibility could be employed as a protective material in telecommunication; chip sets manufacturing, defense and medical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Unwanted noise or interference in an electrical route or circuit brought on by an external source is known as electromagnetic interference (EMI). Radio frequency interference is another name for it. Electronics may perform erratically, malfunction, or stop functioning altogether as a result of EMI (Merizgui et al., 2019). In order to extend the life and functionality of electrical and electronic devices, it is necessary to reduce EMI concerns (Rana et al., 2022). This action might be helpful to increase the technical utility of the gadgets because modern technology is hardly dependent on electrical and electronic devices (Arun Prakash and Viswanathan, 2018). There are four different forms of electromagnetic interference, depending on the source. These categories include deliberate EMI, inadvertent EMI, intra-system EMI, and intersystem EMI. This EMI is purposefully produced by the apparatus, whereas conducted EMI is brought on by the actual physical contact between the conductors. Usually EMI is created by radiation for higher frequencies and conduction for lower frequencies. There are several ways to get rid of it, lessening the negative consequences (Rajadurai, 2016). Filtering, grounding, and shielding are three distinct ways to assist decrease or eliminate the EMI effect. Filtering out undesirable signals is a direct method of doing away with them. Passive filters are effective in this situation and are typically employed in new equipment to reduce EMI. Another important technique that is the subject of current research is shielding. Since shielding is a more straightforward and efficient method of EMI arresting than other methods, Thin polymeric materials with doped or un-doped conditions are utilized in shielding methods to reduce EMI effect. These doped polymeric shielders were capable of efficiently shielding electronic equipment from EM radiation (Song et al., 2014).

However, because different applications demand different types of EMI shielding materials, it may be difficult to design EMI guarding materials that are functionally sound for varied technical uses. These EMI protective materials must not only have good EMI effects, but also be able to tolerate external stresses and have a flexible design in order to be used in difficult applications (Azman et al., 2020). For this reason usually the polymeric materials are reinforced with synthetic or natural fibers and fillers (Syamimi et al., 2020; Faiza, 2021). Since the majority of EMI shielding materials need to cover up the devices in order to stop EM waves from penetrating, they must be extremely flexible. Due to their flexible molecular structure, polymeric materials are therefore frequently employed for this purpose (Prakash et al., 2019). However, thermoset resins are not appropriate for applications requiring flexible EMI shielding. They have very limited usefulness in EMI shielding since they are stiff and brittle. However, scientists are still working on EMI shielding materials based on thermoplastic polymers for a variety of purposes. Numerous studies on thermoplastic polymeric shielding composites made of carbon, CNTs, graphene, and other conducting materials are now being conducted. PVA is a great, extremely flexible polymer that is frequently utilized for packaging and light-weight applications in this line-up. It’s extremely low tensile strength and modulus make them more ideal for straightforward and low-load bearing qualities. Additionally, this PVA is employed as a matrix in several studies based on EMI shielding materials. Flexible Ti3C2Tx/(aramid nanofiber/PVA) composite films were created by Zhang et al. (Zhang et al., 2022) for the best possible electromagnetic interference shielding. In this study, freeze-drying was used to create and characterize Ti3C2T/(ANF/PVA) shielding composites. The study's findings indicate that the Ti3C2T/ANF structure in PVA composite has a total EMI shielding effect of 46 dB. Similar to this, Yu et al. (Yu et al., 2021) used silver nanowires (Ag NWs) and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) as conductive fillers to generate high-performance EMI shielding composite films with outstanding mechanical characteristics. The film, which has a thickness of around 20 m, exhibits exceptional EMI shielding performance (33 dB) and a unique EMI shielding efficiency of 16,800 dB cm1 at 10 GHz, according to the data. The mechanical, electrical, thermal, and tribological characteristics of VA/ MLG/ MWCNT hybrid composites employed in X band EMI shielding application were also examined by Joseph et al. (2020). The filler loadings employed in this work to create composites were 20 wt% MLG, 17.5 wt% MLG / 2.5 wt% MLG, 15 wt% MLG / 5 wt% MWCNT, and 10 wt% MLG / 10 wt% MWCNT. The EMI shielding efficacy of the 2 mm hot pressed multilayered hybrid composite (10 wt% MLG / 10 wt% MWCNT) was reported to be 39 dB in the X-band. Similar to this, several studies on PVA demonstrate that the polymer is extremely feasible for creating these sorts of shielding materials with high economy indices (Joseph et al., 2020). However, due to their combination of magnetic and electrical characteristics, doped PVA-based composites are receiving more attention in the field of EMI shielding. Due to the EM wave's electrical and magnetic components, hitting either one might result in significant shielding. Therefore, several earlier studies have used hybrid fillers. To increase the effectiveness of the shielding, both magnetic and electrical fillers might be chosen in this. Very few researches have examined the impact of hybrid fillers that combine magnetic and electrical properties. There are many types of fillers that are employed as EMI blocking components, including CNTs, charcoal, graphene, cobalt, WC, and Fe2O3. Similarly, fibers are frequently employed for enhanced mechanical qualities. The carbon dots are a carbon-rich, highly crystalline, sustainable substance among fillers (Joseph et al., 2020). Its electrical conductivity and highly crystalline structure encourage its use in EMI shielding applications. Due to their high conductivity compared to other carbon allotropes, which are semi-conductive by nature, C-dots are among the carbon allotropes that are best suited for EMI shielding. The use of biochar in EMI shielding has been revealed in more research than C-dots, which normally have fewer investigations. The suppliers of C-dots claim that wastes from agriculture are frequently employed in their production. The main sources of these C-dots are lignocellulosic materials such grape husk, maize husk, rice husk, wheat husk, and rice husk. Other industrial wastes based on crops are also accessible to produce such C-dots for technological uses. The significant sources for C-dots manufacturing are hemp stalks, bamboo stalks, rubber seed husks, cotton husks, and jatropha seed husks (Rana, 2021; Rana, 2022). Given that the rubber seed husk in this situation has a high carbon content of up to 32%, it may produce a significant quantity of C-dots. Similar to this, a wide variety of fibers, including jute, bamboo, hemp, sisal, and ramie, are utilized to create flexible composites with high hardness (Hanny, 2019; Razi and Islam, 2019). By cutting these fibers with any mechanical technique, they might be employed at the microscopic level. The bamboo among them is less expensive and has a high cellulose content of 60–65%; as a result, this fiber might increase the toughness of composite without impairing its flexibility (Jawaid, 2021). Cobalt also has significant magnetization, retention, and coercivity characteristics, making it a good candidate for magnetic filler.

Accordingly, using C-dots and microfiber might result in increased load bearing and EMI shielding characteristics based on the prior research. Additionally, the PVA matrix is simpler to create and extremely versatile. Despite the fact that there has been a lot of research on biochar, CNTs, graphene, and other metallic fillers, no studies have used rubber seed C-dots with cobalt magnetic fillers. Additionally, it hasn't yet been done to include bamboo microfiber into PVA together with C-dots and cobalt compound particles. In order to close this research gap, the current study was undertaken. In this work, rubber seed husk C-dots and bamboo microfibre were used to create a new flexible PVA composite. To determine the efficacy of the innovative addition, it is also possible to study the behavior of the materials' mechanical, dielectric, magnetic, and EMI shielding properties. Flexible polymeric composites with increased EMI shielding qualities might be used to the telecommunications sector, telecommunication devices, radar, defense, and automobile sectors.

In this study the MERCK, India, Ltd. supplied the water-soluble flexible polyvinyl alcohol powder as matrix. The polymer has a molecular weight of 44.12 g/mol and a density of 1.19 g/cm3. Sigma Aldrich in the United States provided the surface modifier (APTMS) that was utilized to modify the reinforcement's surfaces. Similarly, the 3 µm cobalt particle and the 70 µm dia, 1 mm long bamboo microfibre, all of which were acquired from Chenchem in Chennai, India. The 15 nm rubber seed C-dots, however, were made using by self. The rubber seed wastes were bought from Metro Composites in Chennai, India, and were already treated to get rid of the impurities.

Before the manufacturing of C-dots, the used rubber seed hulls are washed many times and dried. The oven-dried rubber seed hulls are further pyrolyzed in a controlled oxygen flow furnace at a lower temperature of 300 °C, burned entirely, and then kept for roughly 2 h at that temperature. The char was then allowed to cool to ambient temperature before being pulverized in a mortar to a fineness of around 15 nm (Kang and Lee, 2019).

Fig. 1 shows the rubber seed husk C-dots preparation route and its FT-IR, XRD and TEM reports. The FTIR spectra indicate the peaks at all hydrocarbon range starting form C-H stretch and bend vibrations. There are no other peaks were detected above the frequency range 1800 cm−1, indicating the only carbon rich structure presence. Similarly, the TEM image of carbon dots indicates highly crystalline crystal structure and the size in the range of 10–15 nm. The same has been confirmed using the XRD graph. A strong peak 19.74°2θ indicates the crystalline carbon dots along with minor carbon peaks at 27.5 °2θ and 40.8 °2θ. There is no other element and their peaks found in the XRD plot, which confirms the purity of carbon dots prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ball milling of Co and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: To form a fused compound structure for the fabrication of the composite, the cobalt and C-dots mixed particles was ball milled for 3 h. The samples were uniformly ground in a high-energy planetary ball mill (RETSCH, Germany).

The ball material utilized was tungsten carbide (WC), and the powder to ball ratio was kept at 1:15. Throughout the milling process, a planetary grindery mill with a speed of 300 rpm was used. The Co/C-dots composite particle's TEM shape is seen in Fig. 2(a). Both particles obviously attach to one another and form a composite structure. Similarly, Fig. 2(b) indicated the selective X-ray diffraction analysis of milled Co/C-dots and the patterns confirms the both crystalline and amorphous nature of indications. This continuous ring around the selected spot is because of uneven diffraction of X-rays due to the mixed atomic structures. The same has been confirmed using XRD plots too. The graph shows peaks of both C-dots and cobalt at their respective 2θ peaks such as 19.92, 27.8 °2θ for C-dots and 41.6, 44.2, 47.1, 51.3, 61.9 °2θ for cobalt. Thus the milling of both C-dots and cobalt formed compound structure, which could be debate in the wave scattering phenomenon.

The flexible water-soluble PVA-based Co/C-dots hybrid composite films are prepared via a solution casting process. In that, the water-polymer ratio was maintained at 4:1 and allowed to completely mix using a sonicator. A required quantity of bamboo microfibre and Co/C-dots are further added into the PVA blend and allowed for curing in a preformed mould. The casted PVA flexible sheets were further allowed to cure at room temperature for about 8 h to form complete processes of film (Xia et al., 2019). The proposed composite and its composition are listed in Table 1. The prepared PVA sheets with Co/C-dots and microfiber is depicted in Fig. 3.

Co/C-dot and PVA hybrid composites' accumulated magnetic moments were examined using a Vibrato Sample Magnetometer (VSM) in Lakeshore, USA. Shield film layers were properly separated, and chip magnetic currents ranging from 5000 G to 15,000 G were applied. At the y-axis, the induced magnetization analysis was captured (emu). However, the Co/C-dots PVA composites' magnetic permeability and electrical permittivity were determined from the scattering characteristics themselves. To calculate the transmission, reflection, and absorption coefficients, the scattering parameters such as S12 and S21 and S11 and S22 that were used. Based on Equation (Merizgui et al., 2019; Rana et al., 2022; Arun Prakash and Viswanathan, 2018; Rajadurai, 2016; Song et al., 2014), it is possible to determine the shielding effectiveness due to reflection (SER), shielding effectiveness due to absorption (SEA), and shielding effectiveness total (SET). The scattering characteristics were discovered using a high frequency vector network analyzer (VNA) with an 8–20 GHz (X and Ku band) range. Finally, ASTM D 638 and D 2240 were used to check the mechanical parameters such as tensile strength, modulus, percent of elongation, and micro hardness. A universal testing machine (FIE, UNITEK 91400, India) was used check the tensile properties with a cross head speed of 1.5 mm/min. Similarly a Bluesteel make Shore-D durometer was used to check the hardness of composites. A needle indentor was allowed to penetrate and the “D” scale of the dial was noted. For obtaining the average result, a minimum of five similar sized samples were examined. The test specimens of the Co/C-dots bamboo fiber PVA composites are displayed in Fig. 4.

The magnetic behavior curve of the Co/C-dots distributed BMF and PVA hybrid composite is shown in Fig. 5. PVA composite curves have a consistent structure, indicating magnetic behavior and good PVA film susceptibility (PBC2-PBC4). The magnetic moments that were collected by using external magnetic fields demonstrate the beneficial interaction of Co/C-dots hybrid specimens with the underlying magnetic field. With a modest retentivity of 276 and − 272 emu and magnetizations of 536 and − 578 emu in the forward and reverse fields, respectively, the PBC1 composite designation describes the material. Additionally, the composite designation PBC1 showed a respectable coercivity of − 2380 and 2260 G in both the forward and reverse fields. However, it was found that the PVA's magnetic characteristics were enhanced when bigger Co/C-dots were added. The composite designation PBC3 exhibits the maximum values of 964 and 972 emu in terms of magnetization, 784 and − 738 emu in terms of retentivity, and − 7277 and 7028 G in terms of coercivity. Comparing this to the PBC1 composite, there has been an improvement of nearly two times. It is observed that when large Co/C-dots are added to the matrix, the "S" curve widens. The increased acquisition and retention of residual magnetic moments are caused by these phenomena. Therefore, a greater magnetic reverse flux is required to bring the internal magnetic fields back to zero.

Additionally, it should be highlighted that the PVA composites, which include bigger Co/C-dots, have a greater coercivity than the basic PBC2. In order to reduce the magnetic moment of the composite to zero, Co particles with a greater magnetic moment require a bigger reverse flux. In the water soluble PVA composite, the inclusion of magnetic particles enhanced the magnetic domains and increased their magnetic susceptibility (Tejwan et al., 2020).

Additionally, it should be mentioned that Fig. 6 depicts the magnetic permeability of composites made with PVA. At lower frequencies, pure PVA often exhibits decreased permeability, which steadily increases as the frequency increases up to 20 GHz. The increased permeability is caused by a greater concentration of residual magnetism inside the composite. A highest permeability of 5.7 was noted for composite designation PBC4 at 20 GHz. Similarly the other composites, which contain Co/C-dots of considerable volume, improved the permeability. Other than PCB0 and PCB1 the Co/C-dots dispersed PVA composites are produced remarkable improvement in the magnetic permeability. This phenomenon would support to block the magnetic component of incoming microwaves (EM waves) t the time of shielding. It is noted that the permeability is increased with increase of frequency. This phenomenon is the reason for large amplitude high frequency EM wave, which could produce more active magnetic moments with in the shielding composite material. Thus the addition of compound Co/C-dots improved the magnetic permeability (Hezma et al., 2019).

Fig. 7 shows the dielectric constant (relative permittivity) and loss of various composite prepared in this present study. At lower frequencies the composites are giving higher dielectric constant that the higher frequencies. This phenomenon is the reason for at lower frequencies the composites get polarized thereby the charges are easily conducted from one phase of composite to other. However when frequency increased the dielectric relaxation is reduced, which subsequently reduced the polarized atomic pair under external frequency. Thus the charges couldn’t flow further and converted as heat. The PVA gives lower dielectric constant of about 4.1 and loss of 0.12. Being water soluble this PVA is likely to conduct electricity at higher frequencies (Ounacer et al., 2021). However the moment added the Co/C-dots and bamboo fiber of significant volume % into the PVA the permittivity is increased. The composite designation PBC4 at 8 GHz and 20 GHz gives group maximum dielectric constant and loss of 9.2 and 7.8, 0.65 and 0.71 respectively. This increment in permittivity is the reason for the presence of C-dots and its high crystalline conductivity nature. The highly aligned carbon atoms in the aromatic ring form could easily conduct electrons around the walls of the hexagonal links. Moreover these C-dots are thermally stable and chemically resistive. This nature prevented the hindrance of being conductance due to the self heat generation at the time of expose to the EM wave radiation. Thus the addition of cobalt and C-dots at a considering volume improved the permittivity of composites, which could be the mandatory mechanism for wave shielding (Das et al., 2021).

The overall wave shielding performance of the PVA hybrid composites is compared in Fig. 8. By enhancing the Co/C-dots compound particles, the suggested shielding composite's overall shielding is greatly improved. The results obtained imply that a high Co/C-dot composition is regarded as an important factor in improving coherent total microwave shielding. The PVA composites' acquired magnetic and electrical susceptive character, as a result of their inclusion of both electrically conductive and magnetically susceptive materials, is what accounts for the improvement in the EMI SE mechanism. It is widely acknowledged that electrical conductivity has a substantial impact on EMI shielding properties. The electrical conductivity of the pure PVA composite improved after the development of Co/C-dots. The Co/C-dots based PVA composite has a dramatically higher overall EMI shielding efficacy in the X and Ku band (Zubair et al., 2021). The development of Co/C-dots may result in a large number of interfaces and dipoles, which would cause alternating EM fields to lose EM energy. Additionally, the distinctive arrays might be thought of as a miniature antenna for EM energy attenuation. The primary EMI shielding method would switch from reflection to absorption as a result. Additionally, it should be remembered that permittivity decreases when frequency is increased. This behavior is a result of weak polarization, which is caused by a depressed relaxation time that is specific to the polarization permittivity (Kaur et al., 2021). The molecules in the suggested specimens try to react to the external frequency when the frequency has increased. The molecules are in a non-relaxed scenario when the frequency domain is over their lower relaxation period level, which enhances the EMI phenomena. It should also be remembered that as frequency rises, protection from absorption and reflection gets stronger. Increased residual magnetic flux as a result of this increase may enhance the absorption wave shielding process. Thus, higher frequency ranges experience better EM wave shielding than lower frequency ranges. Additionally, the inclusion of Co/C-dots compound particles had a significant impact on both the overall EMI shielding and wave scattering properties (Devi et al., 2022). Amutha et al. (Jeevakumari, 2022) have developed a PVA based EMI shielding composite using Cocos nucifera husk biocarbon and FeSi3. Authors reported a recorded total EMI shielding of 48.4 dB at 20 GHz. However this value is about 11.67% lesser than the present composite’s EMI shielding effectiveness. Same way Sivakumar et al. (2022) also developed a peanut husk carbon quantum dots-epoxy based EMI shielding material in line with ferrite foil. The composites were tested at high frequencies to check their effectiveness. Authors reported an outperformed EMI shielding effectiveness of 54.7 dB at 20 GHz. But when compare with the present material this is a step lesser in EMI shielding though author of the comparative study used epoxy thermoset. The addition of hybrid form of cobalt along with carbon dots are the reason for this higher EMI shielding effectiveness. Similarly, another study by Anju (2019) reported the EMI shielding effectiveness of PANI based EMI shielding material at a low thickness of 0.11 mm at “X” band frequency. Author used conducting matrix to improve the EMI shielding effect than the ordinary polymer matrix. According to results the composite produced EMI shielding which is almost equal to the present composite material structure.

Co/C-dots and bamboo microfibre reinforced PVA composites' mechanical characteristics, including tensile strength, modulus, percent elongation, and hardness, are shown in Table 2. The tensile strength, modulus, and hardness of the bamboo-PVA matrix were reported to be improved by the addition of Co/C-dots, but the percentage of elongation recorded somewhat lower values than pure PVA. This improvement was made possible by the load participation, high Co/C-dot and BMF content (PBC4 designation), and PVA (Lekshmi et al., 2021). The addition of Co/C-dots and BMF to PVA reduces the amount of concentrated stress, increasing the material's tensile strength and modulus. By lying perpendicular to the fracture propagation direction, microfibers prevent crack start. As a result, the fissures eventually stopped growing. However, it should be noted that the remarkable elongation characteristic of the PVA matrix decreased with the addition of Co/C-dots and BMF. The softer molecular structure became moderately harder because the composite got mechanically stronger. Thus, the percentage of elongation is decreasing relative to pristine PVA (Alshahrani and Prakash, 2022). Even with the addition of reinforcements that increase strength, the flexibility is still very much present. Fig. 9 shows the real time composite strip product made using Co/C-dots and bamboo microfiber with PVA. The strip is easily bend and flexible for any kind of engineering applications. Similarly, the addition of Co/C-dots and bamboo into the PVA composite improved the hardness. The improvement in hardness was because of effective filling of voids in the polymer (Alshahrani, 2022). Thus the molecular stretching at the time of load acting is restricted, which improved the hardness. Babu et al. (2022) researched the effect of adding tunicated onion bulb biochar, cobalt and carbon fiber on PVA based EMI shielding composite. Author reported a decent increase in the EMI shielding efficiency after the addition of biocarbon. Same time the mechanical properties also revealed by the authors. According to the results, the addition of reinforcements reduced the % of elongation. But on this present research the % of elongation was not reduced less than 128–130%. This indicates still the PVA composite retain its flexibility. Moreover, Zeng et al. (Zeng, 2020) developed a foldable waste leather based PVA composite for EMI shielding application. Authors reported ever highest shielding effectiveness of 111.3 dB for their multilayer PVA composite. In terms of flexibility the present composite is superior than the foldable waste leather based PVA composite. The presence of cobalt and carbon fiber finely maintains the flexibility. Similarly, Singh et al. (Singh and Kulkarni, 2014) investigated the effect of adding Fe2O3, ZnO, SiO2, ZrO2 and TiO2 nanometallic particle infused PVA composite as EMI shielding material in the C and X band. Author reported a superior EMI shielding effectiveness of − 41.90 dB at X band for the metallic particle infused PVA composite. However author reported the decrement in % of elongation after the addition of these nanomaterials. But in the present study the composites still holds the sufficient flexibility (128.7%) and good EMI shielding effect.

Fig. 10 (a-i) shows the scanning electron microscope image of bamboo microfiber and Co/C-dots toughened PVA composite. Fig. 10(a) shows the pure PVA. The matrix is possesses with larger striations, this lines are possibly occurred at the time of curing (Siddique et al., 2021). The Fig. 10(b) shows the 30 vol% of BMF in PVA matrix. The fiber is uniformly dispersed and there are no clustered form of fiber is seen. The fiber produced highly reacted phase, which is possibly due to the pore-polymer interaction at the time of composite formation.

Similarly, Fig. 10 (c) shows the fiber dispersion at 10 µm scale. The fiber dispersion is more uniform in this wider area coverage (Alshahrani and Prakash, 2022). Moreover, Fig. 10 (d) shows the presence of both fiber and Co/C-dots in PVA. Both the particle and fiber is highly reacted with matrix and posses high adherence. There is no super saturation of particles observed. The same is again confirmed in Fig. 10 (e). The particles are highly reacted with PVA. There is a definite boundary exist between the particle and matrix.

It is further noted that the Fig. 10 (f) shows the dispersion of Co/C-dots in PVA. The particles are neatly aligned and exposed to the outer surface of the PVA composite. Finally Fig. 10 (g-i) shows the tensile fractograph of PBC4 composite. The fractograph shows high ductile fracture and holding the traces of tearing plastic deformation (Merizgui, 2021). The matrix is elongated and tear off at the time of failure happened. There are larger traces of microcrack generation of around the reinforcement in the matrix. This is possibly due to the uniform dispersion of load across the matrix. Thus the addition of both BMF and Co/C-dots into the PVA improved the load bearing phenomenon of flexible PVA composite (Prakash et al., 2019; Rajadurai, 2016).

In this research the EMI shielding measurement of flexible bamboo microfiber and Co/C-dots toughened PVA composites were done. The C-dots were prepared from the husks of rubber seeds whereas the cobalt and bamboo microfibers were as-received. The flexible PVA based EMI shielding composites were prepared via solution casting method and cured. According to the results the excellent electrical conductivity and magnetic susceptive nature of the bamboo microfiber and Co/C-dots hybrid composite the EMI shielding measurement are increased. A highest EMI shielding of − 54.3 dB was noted for composite designation PBC4 at Ku band frequency (20 GHz). Thus it is clear that the composites produced high shielding effect at higher frequencies. Moreover the presence of microfiber in the matrix produced higher load bearing properties thereby the composites are mechanically stronger. Even while the composite has more strength, it still has a great degree of flexibility. It was discovered through study of the entire microwave shielding that EMI blocking is mostly caused by absorption and reflection, which was boosted by the inclusion of BMF and Co/C-dots. Absorption wave losses are caused by interfacial polarization, conduction loss, dipole polarization, and magnetic loss. This success inspires a unique design method for flexible hybrid films with high-driven EMI shielding made of BMF and Co/C-dots. This high EMI shielding measurement and wave blocking phenomena may be helpful in robotics, communications, aviation, and defense applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/NRP/SERC/11/12).
